content:
5 min read
Just now

--

The estimate said 7–8 engineers, 7 months, about $600,000. I shipped it myself in 2 weeks, for about $8,000 in AI usage fees, without writing the code by hand. It’s live and taking payments.
I’m not an engineer. I run a management consulting practice (ex-Accenture strategy), and I built this alongside my day job — roughly half of each day, for two weeks.
This series is my build log: real numbers from actual billing, what worked, and the parts that failed. Nothing to sell you — no course, no template pack.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

What I built
InteriorCapsule: upload one photo of your room, pick a style, and AI redesigns the same room. Numbered tags appear on the furniture, and each one links to a similar real product chosen from a 46,000-item catalog. A makeover takes about 90 seconds. The English version just went live — your first two makeovers are free, no sign-up: https://interiorcapsule.com/en/
Behind that simple flow there is a real system: room-understanding models, an image-generation pipeline, a product-matching engine over the catalog, payments, and a PWA front end. That scope is what made the traditional quote so large.
Where the $600,000 estimate came from
Before building anything, I asked Claude to scope the traditional path — yes, the same AI that later built it. The estimate: 54 person-months. A team of 7–8 engineers for about 7 months. $300,000-$430,000 in salaries alone, over $600,000 as an outsourced contract.
And to be fair to that estimate: this is not a landing page. The finished product is 44,000 lines of product code, 1,449 automated tests, 96 API endpoints, and 15+ external integrations across payments, e-commerce APIs, and image-generation infrastructure.
One caveat I want on the record: I’m comparing an estimate with an actual. Estimates are soft. But this gap is not the kind you argue away with padding.
What actually happened
Idea: June 23. Public beta: July 6. Production launch: July 10. 1,081 commits in 11 days (peak: 156 in a single day). 120 UI releases. My hands-on time: about 0.3 person-months. Total AI bill: about $8,000.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Against the estimate, that is roughly 1/170th of the effort and 1/90th of the cost.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The three things that shocked me more than the numbers
(1) I woke up, and it was done
I ran up to 10 AI coding sessions in parallel, each owning a domain like departments in a small company: one on the product catalog, one on UI, one on production ops, one on QA, one on marketing assets. Each had its own charter and a shared to-do file, and they filed requests to each other through an inbox — like an actual back office.
The thing that quietly disappeared was “waiting for people.” A session that finished at 3 a.m. left a report; the next one picked it up. My morning routine became reading overnight reports from my own company. The only bottleneck left was me.
(2) An admin console in 15 minutes
I described what I wanted from an admin console — search, moderation, bulk edits — and had a working one 15 minutes later. In my consulting life, I have watched that be a multi-month project. When building stops being expensive, you stop rationing your ideas — that changed how I decide what to build far more than the invoice did.
(3) A hundred synthetic users tested the app overnight
I had 100 AI persona agents — each with an age, a living situation, a budget — use the real app end to end. One round came back with 155 findings and an average satisfaction score of 2.85 out of 5, with screenshots attached. A few of the findings (these are AI personas, not real customers):
“There’s no credit-balance indicator. It just cut me off on my fourth makeover.” — AI test user: female, 34, regional city
“It proposed a 6.5-foot sofa for a studio apartment. Physically impossible.” — AI test user: male, 29, lives alone in a studio, big city
“The purchase screen froze. I was tired, so I deleted the app.” — AI test user: female, 30, a nurse, big city
That last one stung because it was right: the purchase screen really did have a freeze bug. Time from finding to fix: hours, not sprints.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

This is not a story about development getting cheaper
The real change is where the bottleneck lives. Code stopped being the scarce resource. My job stopped being “the builder” and became something closer to running a company whose employees are AI: writing the org chart, the rules, the quality gates — and deciding what should exist at all.
That’s what the rest of this series is about: how I structured the AI into a 10-department “company” (and development stopped stalling), what 12.3 billion tokens were actually spent on (95% was neither “thinking” nor “writing”), and the AI’s 10 worst failures — plus what only a human could do.
I’m posting the build in public on X (@interiorcapsule) — if you’re a founder wondering whether this way of building is real, follow along. I’ll keep publishing the receipts.
Try InteriorCapsule (free, no sign-up): https://interiorcapsule.com/en/
I’m Kazuya Uchiyama, founder of InteriorCapsule and a management consultant — not an engineer. Thanks for reading.
